pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Science of the Total Environment 583 (2017) 393–400 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Science of the Total Environment journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv Concentrations and patterns of perﬂuoroalkyl and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances in a river and three drinking water treatment plants near and far from a major production source Virginie Boiteux, Xavier Dauchy ⁎, Cristina Bach, Adeline Colin, Jessica Hemard, Véronique Sagres, Christophe Rosin, Jean-François Munoz ANSES, Nancy Laboratory for Hydrology, Water Chemistry Department, 40 rue Lionnois, 54000 Nancy, France H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • River and well ﬁelds were contaminated by the release of PFASs from a facility. • A poorly studied PFAS (6:2 FTAB) was the predominant PFAS found in the river. • The oxidative conversion method showed evidence for the presence of PFCA precursors. • Conventional water treatments were unable to remove PFASs efﬁciently. • Ozonation and activated carbon ﬁltration may lead to the breakdown of certain FTs. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: Perﬂuoroalkyl and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) are emerging contaminants that have been Received 20 October 2016 detected in the environment, biota and humans. Drinking water is a route of exposure for populations Received in revised form 11 January 2017 using water contaminated by PFAS discharges. This research entailed measuring concentrations, mass Accepted 12 January 2017 ﬂows and investigating the fate of dozens PFASs in a river receiving efﬂuents from a ﬂuorochemical Available online 20 January 2017 manufacturing facility. To measure the total concentration of perﬂuoroalkyl carboxylic acid (PFCA) Editor: D. Barcelo precursors, an oxidative conversion method was used. Several dozen samples were collected in the river (water and sediment), in drinking water resources and at different treatment steps on four sampling Keywords: dates. One PFCA and three ﬂuorotelomers (FTs) were detected up to 62 km downstream from the PFAS manufacturing facility. 6:2 Fluorotelomer sulfonamide alkylbetaine (6:2 FTAB) was the predominant Drinking water treatment PFAS with a mass ﬂow of 3830 g/day 5.2 km downstream from the facility. At all sampling points, PFAS Surface water concentrations in sediment were quite low (b 6 ng/g dw). Five of the 11 investigated wells showed Groundwater detectable concentrations of PFASs. Interestingly, their proﬁle patterns were different from those Sediment observed in the river, suggesting a transformation of PFCA precursors in the sediments of alluvial groundwater. Conventional drinking water treatments (aeration, sand or granular activated carbon ﬁltration, ozonation or chlorination) did not efﬁciently remove PFASs. Furthermore, an increase in Abbreviations: DWTP, drinking water treatment plant; FT, ﬂuorotelomer; FTSA, ﬂuorotelomer sulfonic acid; FTOH, ﬂuorotelomer alcohol; GC–MS, gas chromatography/mass spectrometry; LC-MS/MS, liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry; PFAA, perﬂuoroalkyl acid; PFAS, perﬂuoroalkyl and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances; PFCA, perﬂuoroalkyl carboxylic acid; PFSA, perﬂuoroalkane sulfonic acid. ⁎ Corresponding author. E-mail address: xavier.dauchy@anses.fr (X. Dauchy). http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2017.01.079 0048-9697/© 2017 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

Sammut a, E. Sinagra a,⁎, R. Helmus b, P. de Voogt b,c a Department of Chemistry, University of Malta, Malta b IBED, University of Amsterdam, Amsterdam, Netherlands c KWR, Watercycle Research Institute, Nieuwegein, Netherlands H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • The occurrence and distribution of 2 perﬂuorosulfonates and 5 perﬂuoro- carboxylates in the Maltese surface and rain water were investigated. • PFOA and PFOS were the most abun- dant PFASs in surface water. • Except for a few samples PFAS concen- trations and PFAS composition in pre- cipitation were quite uniform suggesting common sources. • Air masses from source regions yielded rain with higher ƩPFAS concentrations than ones travelling over sea or having rain events prior to sampling. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: The presence of perﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) in rain water on the Maltese Islands is reported here for the Received 13 September 2016 ﬁrst time and an extensive survey of these substances in surface water also reported. The Maltese archipelago Received in revised form 15 February 2017 lies at the centre of the Mediterranean Sea and consists of three main inhabited islands: Malta, Gozo and Comino. Accepted 15 February 2017 Surface water was collected from 41 valleys during the months of February and March 2015 at the peak of the Available online 2 March 2017 wet season. Rain water was collected during the months of December 2014, February, August, September and Oc- Editor: D. Barcelo tober 2015. PFASs were extracted from the water samples using solid phase extraction and the extracts were then analysed using ultra performance liquid chromatography coupled to mass spectrometry in tandem (UPLC-MS/ Keywords: MS). All surface and rain water samples were contaminated with at least one PFAS. PFOS (bLOD – 8.6 ng/L) Perﬂuoroalkyl substances and PFOA (ND – 16 ng/L) were the two major PFASs being detected in 100% and 95% of the surface waters respec- Malta tively. The ΣPFAS concentrations in rain water ranged between 0.38 ng/L (1st October 2015) and 6 ng/L (21st Surface water February 2015). The Maltese archipelago is surrounded by sea and disconnected from any other mainland; there- Rain water fore the results conﬁrm that remote environments can become contaminated by PFASs from rain events depend- ing on wind prevailing trajectories. © 2017 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. ⁎ Corresponding author at: Room 109, Chemistry & Pharmacology Building, University of Malta, Msida. E-mail address: emmanuel.sinagra@um.edu.mt (E. In the gas Accepted 19 April 2017 phase, the concentrations of neutral ∑PFASs were within the range of 76–551 pg/m3. Perﬂuorooctanoic acid (PFOA) was the Yellow Sea predominant compound accounting for 51%–90% of the ionic ∑PFAS concentrations. Releases from industrial PFOA and domestic activities as well as the semiclosed geographical conditions increased the level of ionic ∑PFASs in the Bohai Sea. pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Science of the Total Environment 607–608 (2017) 1201–1212 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Science of the Total Environment journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv Analysis of emerging organic contaminants in water, ﬁsh and suspended particulate matter (SPM) in the Joint Danube Survey using solid-phase extraction followed by UHPLC-MS-MS and GC–MS analysis Robert Loos ⁎, Simona Tavazzi, Giulio Mariani, Gert Suurkuusk, Bruno Paracchini, Gunther Umlauf European Commission, Joint Research Centre (JRC), Directorate D – Sustainable Resources, Water and Marine Resources, I-21027 Ispra, VA, Italy H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • 71 water samples from the Danube River and its tributaries were analysed. • Most relevant micropollutants were benzotriazoles, pharmaceuticals, organ- ophosphorus compounds, and PFOS/A. • PFOS concentrations exceed its environ- mental quality standard (EQS). • Concentrations and loads are similar for the years 2007 and 2013. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: In the third Joint Danube Survey (JDS3), emerging organic contaminants were analysed in the dissolved water Received 16 May 2017 phase of samples from the Danube River and its major tributaries. Analyses were performed using solid-phase ex- Received in revised form 4 July 2017 traction (SPE) followed by ultra-high-pressure liquid chromatography triple-quadrupole mass spectrometry Accepted 4 July 2017 (UHPLC-MS-MS) and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS). The polar organic compounds analysed Available online 17 July 2017 by UHPLC-MS-MS were 1H-benzotriazole, methylbenzotriazoles, carbamazepine, 10,11-dihydro-10,11-dihy- Keywords: droxy-carbamazepine, diclofenac, sulfamethox-azole, 2,4-D (2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid), MCPA (2-methyl- Danube River 4-chlorophenoxyacetic acid), metolachlor, cybutryne (irgarol), terbutryn, DEET (N,N-diethyl-m-toluamide), and Water monitoring several perﬂuoroalkyl acids (C6–C9; C8 = perﬂuorooctanoic acid (PFOA)) and perﬂuorooctansulfonic acid UHPLC-MS-MS (PFOS). In addition, several organophosphorus ﬂame retardants were analysed by GC-MS. The most relevant com- GC-MS pounds identiﬁed in the 71 water samples, in terms of highest median and maximum concentrations, were 1H- Water Framework Directive benzotriazole, tris(1-chloro-2-propyl)phosphate (TCPP), methylbenzotriazoles, carbama-zepine and its metabo- Emerging organic contaminants lite, DEET, sulfamethoxazole, tris(isobutyl)phosphate (TiBP), tris(2-chloroethyl)phosphate (TCEP), PFOA, PFOS and diclofenac. The concentrations of these compounds in the samples were generally below the environmental quality standard (EQS) threshold values, with the exception of PFOS, the concentration of which exceeded the an- nual average water EQS limit of 0.65 ng/L along the whole river, and also exceeded the ﬁsh biota EQS of 9.1 μg/kg. It was Accepted 22 August 2017 found that PFHxS, instead of PFOS or PFOA, was predominant both in the Lake and rivers (45.9–351 ng/L), Available online 1 September 2017 reﬂecting increasing demand of PFHxS in recent years. The percentage of linear (n-) PFOS and the ratio of 3 + 5m-/1m-PFOS were combined to in- dicate indirect source due to biodegradation of PFOS-precursors. The percentage order of n-PFOS was: the Lake Keywords: (48.7%) b the ﬂow-in rivers (59.2%) b ECF product (70.3%). These suggested Isomers that degradation of PFOS-precursors made distinct contribution to PFOS load in the waters, particularly in the Taihu Lake Lake. pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Science of the Total Environment 627 (2018) 1334–1344 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Science of the Total Environment journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv Distribution of perﬂuorinated compounds (PFASs) in the aquatic environment of the industrially polluted Vaal River, South Africa Thimo Groffen a,⁎, Victor Wepener b, Wynand Malherbe b, Lieven Bervoets a a Systemic Physiological and Ecotoxicology Research (SPHERE), Department of Biology, University of Antwerp, Groenenborgerlaan 171, 2020 Antwerp, Belgium b Unit for Environmental Sciences and Management, North West University, 11 Hoffman Street, 2520 Potchefstroom, South Africa H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • Little is known on PFAS concentrations in South African aquatic ecosystems. • Water, sediment, invertebrates and ﬁsh were sampled along a gradient. • Except for PFOS, PFAS concentrations were low compared to literature. • PFAS concentrations in biota and water showed different gradients. • More research is necessary to under- stand the PFAS distribution in the Vaal River. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: Perﬂuorinated alkyl substances (PFASs) are highly persistent chemicals, which have a bioaccumulative potential Received 4 December 2017 and can be found in wildlife around the world. Although multiple studies have been performed on PFASs pollu- Received in revised form 2 February 2018 tion of the aquatic environment, little is known on PFASs pollution on the African continent and their possible Accepted 2 February 2018 risks for human health. In the present study, we examined the distribution of 15 PFASs in ﬁsh, invertebrates, sed- Available online 20 February 2018 iment and water, collected at three sites, representing a gradient of industrial and mining pollution, along the Editor: Yolanda Picó Vaal River, South Africa. Furthermore, possible risks for human health through consumption of contaminated ﬁsh were examined. Keywords: Perﬂuorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) was the most dominant PFAS measured in biota, whereas perﬂuoropentanoic Perﬂuorinated compounds acid (PFPeA) was measured in higher concentrations in water. Mean PFAS concentrations in water ranged from South Africa bLOQ to 38.5 ng/L. PFAS concentrations in water decreased along the gradient and were similar or lower com- Aquatic environment pared to other studies in Europe, Asia and America. PFAS measurements in sediment were bLOQ, with the excep- PFASs tion of PFOS at Thabela Thabeng (2.36 ng/g dry weight (dw)). Average ∑PFAS concentrations in biota increased Human health along the gradient and ranged from bLOQ to 34.5 ng/g wet weight (ww) in invertebrates, bLOQ to 289 ng/g ww in liver and bLOQ to 34.0 ng/g ww in muscle tissue. Although PFOS concentrations were relatively high compared to literature, concentrations of other PFASs were rather low. A potential risk for humans through consumption of PFAS-contaminated ﬁsh was assessed. Tolerable daily intake values (grams of ﬁsh that can be eaten daily without risking health effects) were much lower than the average South African ﬁsh consumption per day, implying a potential risk for human health through consumption of PFAS contaminated ﬁsh. ⁎ Corresponding author. Bervoets). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.02.023 0048-9697/© 2018 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

The dominant PFASs were perﬂuorohexanesulfonate (PFHxS) in wastewater, perﬂuorooctanoate PFAS (PFOA) in surface water, perﬂuorooctanesulfonate (PFOS) in soil and perﬂuoroheptanoate (PFHpA) and PFOA in PFOS, PFOA different plant tissues, reﬂecting PFAS-speciﬁc partitioning behaviour in different matrices. Soil-water Soil partitioning coefﬁcient (log Kd) in wetland soil under yam was lowest for short-chain PFHxA (1.9–2.3 L kg−1) Wastewater and increased with increasing chain length to 2.8–3.1 L kg−1 for perﬂuoroundecanoate (PFUnDA) and Maize 2.8–3.1 L kg−1 for perﬂuoroctanesulfonate (PFOS). The log Koc values ranged between 2.2 and 3.6 L kg−1, with Yam Sugarcane the highest log Koc estimated for long-chain perﬂuorocarbon PFASs (i.e. PFUnDA 3.2–3.5 L kg−1 and PFOS 3.2–3.6 L kg−1). The concentration ratio (CR) between plants and soil was b1 for all PFASs and plant species, with the highest CR estimated for PFHpA (0.65–0.67) in sugarcane stem and PFBS (0.53–0.59) in yam root. ⁎ Corresponding author. pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Science of the Total Environment 634 (2018) 251–259 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Science of the Total Environment journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv Occurrence, partitioning and bioaccumulation of emerging and legacy per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances in Taihu Lake, China Meng Chen a,1, Qiang Wang a,1, Guoqiang Shan a, Lingyan Zhu a,b,⁎, Liping Yang a, Menglin Liu a a Key Laboratory of Pollution Processes and Environmental Criteria, Ministry of Education, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Environmental Remediation and Pollution Control, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, PR China b College of Natural Resources and Environment, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shanxi 712100, PR China H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • Emerging PFASs were measured in water, sediment, and organisms from Taihu Lake. • 6:2 diPAP, NEtFOSAA and 6:6 PFPiA were frequently detected in the sedi- ment samples. • As an alternative of PFOS, PFHxS pre- dominated in the waters. • The log KOC and log BAF of some emerg- ing PFASs were lower than expected. • Similar to PFOA and PFOS, n-PFHxS pre- ferred to be enriched than br-isomers. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: There are many studies about the occurrence of legacy perﬂuoroalkyl acids (PFAAs) in fresh water ecosystem, but Received 19 January 2018 related information about emerging per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) is limited. In this study, along Received in revised form 22 March 2018 with ten legacy PFAAs, twelve non-PFAAs, including precursors of perﬂuorooctane sulfonic acid (PFOS) and emerg- Accepted 24 March 2018 ing PFASs in the water, sediment and organisms in Taihu Lake, China, were investigated. As one replacement product Available online 5 April 2018 of PFOS, perﬂuorohexane sulfonic acid (PFHxS) (mean: 69.3 ng/L) was the predominant PFAA in the water samples. Editor: Jay Gan Perﬂuorooctanesulfonamide (PFOSA) (mean: 0.190 ng/L) and 6:2 ﬂuorotelomer phosphate diester (6:2 diPAP) (0.034 ng/L) were detected in all the water samples. Other emerging PFASs, such as N-ethyl Keywords: perﬂuorooctanesulfonamidoa-cetic acid (NEtFOSAA), bis(perﬂuorohexyl) phosphinic acid (6:6 PFPiA) were fre- Per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances quently detected in the sediment and/or organisms. The organic carbon normalized sediment-water distribution co- Precursors efﬁcients (KOC) and bioaccumulation factors (BAFs) of the PFASs generally increased with their log KOW values. DiPAPs However, PFOSA, NEtFOSAA, 6:2 diPAP and 6:6 PFPiA displayed lower log BAFs, and/or lower log KOC than expected, Sediment water partition coefﬁcient which may be due to biotransformation potentials and/or large molecular size. PFOSA was not biomagniﬁed in the Bioaccumulation food web. For other emerging PFASs, the low detection frequencies in the organisms hampered us to evaluate their biomagniﬁcation potentials. The lower percentage of most of the branched PFOS isomers while higher percentage of 1 m-PFOS in the organisms suggested that there was indirect source of PFOS in the organisms. Similar to PFOS and PFOA, linear PFHxS isomer was preferentially enriched in organisms relative to its branched isomers. © 2018 Elsevier B.V. pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Science of the Total Environment 651 (2019) 1670–1679 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Science of the Total Environment journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv Impact of on-site wastewater inﬁltration systems on organic contaminants in groundwater and recipient waters Qiuju Gao a, Kristin M. Blum a, Pablo Gago-Ferrero b, Karin Wiberg b, Lutz Ahrens b, Patrik L. Andersson a,⁎ a Dept. of Chemistry, Umeå University, SE-901 87 Umeå, Sweden b Dept. of Aquatic Sciences and Assessment, Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences (SLU), SE-756 55 Uppsala, Sweden H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • Organic contaminants were found in groundwater affected by on-site inﬁltra- tion. • Attenuation of organic contaminants depended on the site's hydrogeology. • Contaminants with high hydrophobicity and sorption potential showed higher attenuation. • Individual compounds showed signiﬁ- cant seasonal variations. • Some contaminants posed a potential risk to organisms in nearby surface water. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: On-site sewage treatment facilities, particularly septic systems combined with soil inﬁltration, can be an impor- Received 1 August 2018 tant source of emerging organic contaminants in groundwater and surface water and thus represent a signiﬁcant Received in revised form 1 October 2018 source of environmental and human exposure. Two inﬁltration systems in Åre municipality, Sweden, were exam- Accepted 1 October 2018 ined to assess the occurrence of contaminants in groundwater and their fate and transport during inﬁltration. Available online 3 October 2018 Groundwater samples, recipient surface water samples, and wastewater samples from septic tanks were col- Editor: Patricia Holden lected from 2016 to 2017 covering all climatological seasons. These samples were analysed for a total of 103 con- taminants, including pharmaceuticals, personal care products, organic phosphorus ﬂame-retardants, plasticisers, Keywords: perﬂuoroalkyl substances, and food additives. Fourteen of 103 contaminants showed 100% detection frequency Micropollutants in groundwater at concentrations in the low ng L−1 to low μg L−1 range. Of the compounds analysed, tris Environmental fate (2‑butoxyethyl) phosphate, sucralose, caffeine, and benzophenone showed high abundancy with maximum con- Pharmaceuticals and personal care products centrations in the μg L−1 range. The data were normalised for dilution using chloride and sucralose as commonly Drain ﬁeld applied tracers; however, the level of sucralose decreased signiﬁcantly during inﬁltration and it is thus subopti- Leach ﬁeld mal as a sewage water tracer. Large differences between the two inﬁltration sites were observed in detection fre- Decentralised wastewater treatment system quencies and concentrations in groundwater, which could be attributed to the system design and the contaminant's migration time from release to sampling point. Seasonal variation was observed for selected chemicals, and the more hydrophobic chemicals showed a higher tendency for attenuation, indicating sorption as a major retention mechanism. A moderate environmental risk to aquatic organisms was estimated in adjacent surface water for galaxolide, tris(1‑chloro‑2‑propyl) phosphate, and tris(2‑butoxyethyl) phosphate. Due to this site-dependency and potential environmental risks, further studies are needed on inﬁltration systems in different ⁎ Corresponding author. E-mail address: patrik.andersson@umu.se (P.L. Andersson). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2018.10.016 0048-9697/© 2018 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Science of the Total Environment 660 (2019) 297–305 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Science of the Total Environment journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv Perﬂuoroalkyl substances in the riverine and coastal water of the Beibu Gulf, South China: Spatiotemporal distribution and source identiﬁcation Chang-Gui Pan, Ke-Fu Yu ⁎, Ying-Hui Wang ⁎, Wei Zhang, Jun Zhang, Jing Guo Guangxi Laboratory on the Study of Coral Reefs in the South China Sea, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China Coral Reef Research Center of China, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China School of Marine Sciences, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • A comprehensive survey of PFASs in the Beibu Gulf, China, is reported. • Total PFASs concentrations ranged from 610 to 4920 pg/L in the Beibu Gulf. • PFPeA and PFBA were the dominant PFASs compound, rather than PFOA and PFOS. • The distribution of PFASs was strongly affected by ocean currents. • PFASs would represent a low risk to the aquatic organisms in the Beibu Gulf. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: Few studies have examined the perﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) contamination in less-developed coastal re- Received 4 November 2018 gions. In the present study, we collected 19 riverine and 21 coastal surface water samples in the summer and win- Received in revised form 3 January 2019 ter of 2017 to investigate PFASs contamination in the Beibu Gulf, South China. The results show that eleven and Accepted 3 January 2019 twelve target PFASs were detected in the summer and winter, respectively. The total PFASs (ΣPFASs) concentra- Available online 4 January 2019 tions in the water of the Beibu Gulf were in the range of 1609–4727 pg/L and 610–4920 pg/L in summer and win- Editor: Yolanda Picó ter, respectively. Perﬂuoropentanoic acid (PFPeA), perﬂuorobutanoic acid (PFBA) and perﬂuorobutane sulfonate (PFBS) were the predominantly detected PFASs in both seasons with maximum concentrations of 2968 pg/L, Keywords: 1771 pg/L, and 1764 pg/L, respectively. Strong positive correlations between some PFASs were observed Perﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) (e.g., PFBA and PFBS, PFOS and PFBS, p b 0.05), suggesting these correlated pollutants may share similar sources. Perﬂuorobutane sulfonate (PFBS) PFASs contamination in the Beibu Gulf was strongly affected by ocean currents, and their concentrations were Beibu Gulf lower than most coastal waters around the world. Risk assessment indicates a low risk associated with target Riverine water PFASs to aquatic organisms in the Beibu Gulf. The results of the present research provided a baseline and good Coastal water overview of the spatial distribution of PFASs along the Beibu Gulf. Risk assessment © 2019 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. ⁎ Corresponding authors at: Guangxi Laboratory on the Study of Coral Reefs in the South China Sea, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China. E-mail addresses: kefuyu@gxu.edu.cn, kefuyu@scsio.ac.cn (K.-F. Yu), wyh@gxu.edu.cn (Y.-H. Wang). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2019.01.019 0048-9697/© 2019 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

Sammut a, E. Sinagra a,⁎, M. Sapiano b, R. Helmus c, P. de Voogt c,d a Department of Chemistry, University of Malta, Malta b EWA, Energy and Water Agency, Malta c IBED, University of Amsterdam, Amsterdam, Netherlands d KWR, Watercycle Research Institute, Nieuwegein, Netherlands H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • Occurrence and distribution of 7 perﬂuoroalkyl substances in the Maltese soil, sediment and groundwater were investigated. • PFOS, PFOA and PFDA were the most abundant PFASs in soil and sediment with the highest PFAS concentration al- ways being PFOS. • All PFAS analysed were found in groundwater with PFOA being the most abundant. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: The presence of perﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) in sediments and groundwater on the Maltese Islands is re- Received 10 January 2019 ported here for the ﬁrst time. This study is aimed at investigating the importance of selected emerging PFASs as pollutants in Accepted 23 May 2019 European coastal environments and a possible transition from legacy long-chain PFCAs and PFSAs to replacement Available online 24 May 2019 compounds. Therefore, the spatial distribution of 29 PFASs was analysed in surface water and sediment of the Editor: Patricia Holden North and Baltic Seas sampled in 2017. Levels of the replacement compound HFPO-DA were approximately three times higher than those of its predecessor PFOA in surface water from the North Sea, which is characterised Keywords: by the inﬂuence of point sources and constant exchange with open water. Reanalysis of sample extracts from the Emerging contaminants last decade showed that HFPO-DA had already been present in 2011, when it had not yet been in focus. The emerging cyclic Spatial distribution compound perﬂuoro-4-ethylcyclohexanesulfonate (PFECHS), which has not yet been reported in European Partitioning coastal environments, was detected in 86% of the Baltic Sea samples. Inﬂuenced by sediment characteristics in addition to source-speciﬁc contributions, the spatial distribution of PFASs in surface sediments was more variable than for water samples. The linear isomer of the long-chain legacy substance PFOS was the predominant com- pound found over the entire study area. Of the emerging PFASs, 6:6 and 6:8 perﬂuoroalkyl phosphinic acids (PFPiAs) were identiﬁed close to potential industrial inputs and in sedimentation areas. The results show that particular emerging PFASs play a relevant role in the investigated coastal environments and that a shift to re- placements is dependent on sources and geographical conditions. © 2019 The Authors. pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Science of the Total Environment 695 (2019) 133810 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Science of the Total Environment journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv Brominated ﬂame retardants and perﬂuoroalkyl substances in landﬁll leachate from Ireland Stuart Harrad a,⁎, Daniel S. Drage a, Martin Sharkey b, Harald Berresheim b a School of Geography, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of Birmingham, Birmingham B15 2TT, UK b School of Physics, National University of Ireland Galway, Galway, Ireland H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • PFASs, PBDEs, and HBCDD detected in leachate from Irish landﬁlls • Average concentrations of PFASs exceed those of BFRs. • Concentrations of most PFASs and PBDEs higher in lined than in unlined landﬁlls • Concentrations of some PFASs and PBDEs correlated with leachate COD. • BDE-209 in leachate likely associated with abraded particles from waste articles a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: Between June and November 2017, leachate samples were collected from 40 landﬁlls across the Republic of Received 17 June 2019 Ireland. Concentrations of perﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs), polybrominated diphenyl ethers (PBDEs), and Received in revised form 19 July 2019 hexabromocyclododecane (HBCDD) determined in these samples were within the range previously reported Accepted 5 August 2019 in other countries. Average concentrations of PFASs exceeded those of PBDEs and HBCDD; likely due to the higher Available online 06 August 2019 water solubility of PFASs. Log-transformed concentrations of BDEs-47, 100, 153, and 183, as well as Editor: Adrian Covaci perﬂuorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perﬂuorononanoic acid (PFNA) and perﬂuorobutane sulfonate (PFBS) were sig- niﬁcantly (p b 0.05) higher in leachate from newer, lined landﬁlls than in samples from unlined landﬁlls. These Keywords: higher concentrations in lined landﬁlls are likely related to the fact that lined landﬁlls are found to retain organic PBDEs matter leading to a higher organic content of leachate from such landﬁlls. This is evidenced by the signiﬁcant (p b HBCDD 0.05) correlation between log-transformed concentrations in leachate of most of the same contaminants and PFASs those of chemical oxygen demand (COD). Concentrations of the less water-soluble, higher molecular weight Landﬁll leachate BDE-209 were not correlated with leachate COD, nor landﬁll age or the presence of a landﬁll liner. This suggests that the presence of BDE-209 in landﬁll leachate is driven more by physical abrasion of particles and ﬁbres from waste articles, than dissolution into the aqueous phase. The higher concentrations of some PFASs and PBDEs in leachate from lined landﬁlls present a challenge with respect to leachate disposal, when leachate is sent to waste- water treatment plants that do not necessarily have mechanisms in place to remove or destroy these chemicals prior to discharge into the environment. Moreover, the presence of these persistent organic chemicals in leachate ⁎ Corresponding author. E-mail address: S.J.Harrad@bham.ac.uk (S. The present study was carried out in a drinking water Received in revised form 13 August 2019 treatment plant (DWTP) located in the city of Changzhou (China), near the Yangtze River Delta. 31 PFASs concen- Accepted 15 August 2019 trations were analyzed in the DWTP process water ﬂows, as well as in its upstream source water (Yangtze River Available online 22 August 2019 and Desheng River) and downstream public supply water. 13 PFASs were detected in source and drinking water Editor: Yolanda Picó with total concentration of 25–38 ng/L, which were higher for Yangtze River than Desheng River. The predomi- nant contaminants were perﬂuorobutanesulfonate, perﬂuorooctanoic acid and perﬂuorobutanoic acid. In addi- Keywords: tion, HFPO-TA was identiﬁed for the ﬁrst time in Yangtze River. Data indicate that PFASs discharge in Yangtze PFASs River is remarkable, with a mass ﬂow in the wet season (i.e. summer) up to 83 kg/d. However, oxidizable precur- DWTP sors (quantiﬁed by total oxidizable precursor assay) were 21–88% of total PFASs, constituted mainly by short- Removal rate chain homologues. Taking these precursors into account, an opposite picture is observed: Desheng River had TOP assay higher concentration of total PFASs than Yangtze River. Results for the DWTP indicate that PFASs concentration Health risk assessment was not affected by conventional treatment processes and remained unchanged also in the downstream public water supply pipelines. More importantly, granular activated carbon removed effectively only sulfonated and carboxylated congeners, but was inefﬁcacious with oxidizable precursors. Concentrations of PFASs were only measured in Oreochromis mossambicus from the uMvoti and contained PFOS concentrations ranging from 0.18 to 0.97 ng/g ww in muscle tissue and 7.29–27.96 ng/g ww in liver tissue. PFOA concentrations ran- ged between 0.12 and 0.58 ng/g ww in muscle tissue and 0.17–1.01 ng/g ww in liver tissue. Drage a, Martin Sharkey b, Harald Berresheim b a School of Geography, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of Birmingham, Birmingham B15 2TT, UK b School of Physics, National University of Ireland Galway, Galway, Ireland H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • BFRs and PFASs measured in air, soil and groundwater near 10 Irish landﬁlls. • First report of DBDPE in groundwater at levels exceeding PBDEs, HBCDD and PFOA • BFRs and PFASs in air and soil not im- pacted by landﬁll. • PFOA in groundwater positively corre- lated with concentrations in landﬁll leachate. • PFOA higher in groundwater near un- lined than near lined landﬁlls a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: Between November 2018 and January 2019, samples of air and soil were collected from locations downwind and Received 25 October 2019 upwind of 10 landﬁlls across the Republic of Ireland. Samples of groundwater (n = 10) were also collected from Received in revised form 27 November 2019 locations with links traceable to the studied landﬁlls. Concentrations of perﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs), Accepted 27 November 2019 polybrominated diphenyl ethers (PBDEs), hexabromocyclodecane (HBCDD), and decabromodiphenyl ethane Available online 29 November 2019 (DBDPE) determined in air and soil samples were not signiﬁcantly different (p N 0.05) between downwind Editor: Adrian Covaci and upwind locations. The arithmetic mean concentration of PFOA in groundwater sourced from landﬁlls (n = 4) that were not fully lined (69 ng/L) exceeded that in groundwater samples sourced from lined landﬁlls Keywords: (n = 6; 4.1 ng/L), with the difference, however being not statistically signiﬁcant (p N 0.1). A positive correlation PFASs (p = 0.014) was observed between concentrations of PFOA in groundwater in our study and those reported pre- BFRs viously in leachate from the same landﬁlls. However, this correlation was driven substantially by one landﬁll DBDPE which displayed the highest concentrations in both groundwater and leachate and no signiﬁcant correlation Landﬁll (p N 0.1) was observed between log-transformed concentrations of PFOA in groundwater and leachate. DBDPE Air: Soil was detected in groundwater for the ﬁrst time anywhere, in all samples at concentrations (median = 9.4 ng/L; Groundwater arithmetic mean = 78 ng/L) that exceeded those of any other BFRs or PFASs targeted in this study. This likely re- ﬂects its recent use as a “drop-in” replacement for the recently restricted Decabromodiphenyl ether product. Overall, our data suggest that the 10 landﬁlls studied do not exert a discernible inﬂuence on local air and soil con- centrations of BFRs and PFASs. In contrast, while not of immediate concern, our data suggest that further more detailed study of the impact of landﬁll emissions on concentrations of DBDPE and PFOA is advisable. © 2018 Elsevier B.V. Weber c a School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Water Research Centre, University of New South Wales, Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia b Western Sydney University, Penrith, NSW 2751, Australia c Environmental Sciences Group, Royal Military College of Canada, Kingston, ON, Canada H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • Precursors an important part PFAS mass Rela ve Abundance in source zone Syntrophaceae Oxalobacteraceae Gordonia • PFAS constituents not major drivers of family family microbial community structure • Relative abundance of many individual ? genera were signiﬁcantly associated with PFAS. or • Genera stimulated at low PFAS concen- trations but inhibited at high concentra- tion. 100 1,000 10,000 100,000 10 • Positive correlations between some genera relative abundance and PFAS Total PFAS Concentraon (ng/L) a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: We collected over 40 groundwater samples from a per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFAS) impacted legacy Received 8 October 2019 ﬁre ﬁghting training area in Canada to develop an in-depth assessment of the relationship between PFAS and Received in revised form 28 November 2019 in situ microbial communities. Results suggest differential transport of PFAS of differing chain-length and head Accepted 6 December 2019 group. There is also evidence of PFAS degradation, in particular 6:2 FTS degradation. Received in revised form 30 January 2020 ZR water quality is therefore highly important and directly inﬂuences crop and soil quality in irrigated ﬁelds. Accepted 31 January 2020 This study investigated the occurrence and concentration of 20 per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) in Available online 1 February 2020 Assamra wastewater, ZR water, soils and crop plants (alfalfa (Medicago sativa), mint (Mentha spicata) and lettuce Editor: Shuzhen Zhang (Lactuca sativa)) along the ZR ﬂow path between Assamra WWTP and Jerash spring. The combined PFAS concen- tration (∑PFASs) in Assamra WWTP efﬂuent (14–24 ng L−1) was comparable to that in inﬂuent (10–15 ng L−1), Keywords: indicating poor removal of PFASs. The dominant PFAS in inﬂuent was perﬂuorodecanoate (PFDA), while Agriculture perﬂuorooctanoate (PFOA) and perﬂuoropentanoate (PFPeA) dominated in efﬂuent. ∑PFASs in an unaffected Alfalfa upstream tributary (Sukhna station) was 4.7–5.4 ng L−1. Farther downstream, ZR water contained Plant uptake 16–27 ng L−1, with PFPeA, PFOA and PFDA dominating, and these levels did not change along the ﬂow path to PFAS Jerash spring. ∑PFASs in soil was generally low, 340 ± 150 pg g−1 dry weight (dw) in alfalfa soil (mainly PFOA PFOA and PFDA) and 710 ± 420 pg g−1 dw in mint soil and 970 ± 800 pg g−1 dw in lettuce soil (mainly linear PFDA perﬂuorooctane sulfonate (L-PFOS) in both cases). Soil-water partitioning coefﬁcient (Kd) was generally low in PFPeA Soil all soils (range 24–62 L kg−1, 20–46 L kg−1 and 28 L kg−1 for PFOA, PFDA and L-PFHxS, respectively). No ⁎ Corresponding author. pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Science of the Total Environment 729 (2020) 138753 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Science of the Total Environment journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv Multicompartment occurrence and partitioning of alternative and legacy per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances in an impacted river in China Lijuan Gao, Jingling Liu ⁎, Kun Bao, Nannan Chen, Bo Meng School of Environment, Beijing Normal University, State Key Laboratory of Water Environment Simulation, Beijing 100875, China H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • PFBS and PFBA were predominant in the overlying water, pore water and sedi- ment in the Xi River-Daling River. • Alternatives were observed at higher concentrations in the pore water than in the overlying water. • Cations content had a lower impact on PFAS sediment levels when the organic carbon fraction was high enough. • As with PFOA and PFOS, HFPO-DA and 6:2 Cl-PFESA were prone to transfer from the aqueous phase to the solid phase. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: Per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) are emerging global environmental contaminants. Exploring the occur- Received 21 February 2020 rence and environmental behavior of PFASs in the aquatic environment is a key step in solving global ﬂuorine chem- Received in revised form 10 April 2020 ical pollution problems. In this study, surface water, pore water, and sediment were collected from the main Accepted 15 April 2020 tributary and the middle and lower reaches of the Daling River, adjacent to the Fuxin ﬂuorochemical manufacturing Available online 18 April 2020 facilities in Liaoning Province in China, to elucidate the occurrence and partition behavior of PFASs. The total concen- Editor: Jay Gan trations of PFASs ranged from 48.4 to 4578 ng/L in the overlying water, from 173 to 9952 ng/L in the pore water, and from 2.16 to 40.3 ng/g dw in the sediment fraction. Generally, perﬂuorobutanoic acid (PFBA) and perﬂuorobutane Keywords: sulfonate (PFBS) were the predominant congeners in the samples, with the mean relative content fractions being Perﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) almost consistently N40% in the dissolved phase and N25% in the sediment. Hexaﬂuoropropylene oxide dimer Alternatives acid (HFPO-DA) and chlorinated polyﬂuorinated ether sulfonic acid (6:2 Cl-PFESA) were detected, albeit at low Occurrence levels. In addition, the detection frequency and the contribution of legacy long-chain PFASs in sediment were higher Partitioning than those in the overlying water and pore water. Except for perﬂuorohexane sulfonate (PFHxS), the concentrations Pore water of the alternative PFASs in the pore water were higher than in the overlying water. The organic carbon fraction was a more important controlling factor for PFAS sediment levels than cations content. As with legacy long-chain PFASs, HFPO-DA and 6:2 Cl-PFESA tended to partition into the solid phase, whereas short-chain PFASs were readily distrib- uted in the aqueous phase. Such research results will be helpful in modeling the transport and fate of PFASs released by point sources into coastal waters through rivers and in developing effective risk assessment and management strategies for the control of PFAS pollution. © 2020 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. ⁎ Corresponding author. E-mail address: jingling@bnu.edu.cn (J. Liu). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2020.138753 0048-9697/© 2020 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Science of the Total Environment 751 (2021) 141622 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Science of the Total Environment journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv Perﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in surface water and sediments from two urban watersheds in Nevada, USA Xuelian Bai a,⁎, Yeongkwon Son b a Division of Hydrologic Sciences, Desert Research Institute, Las Vegas, NV 89119, USA b Division of Atmospheric Sciences, Desert Research Institute, Reno, NV 89512, USA H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • Perﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFAS) pose human health and ecological risks. • 17 PFAS were monitored in surface water and sediments from two urban watersheds. • Short-chain PFAS (≤8 carbons) were predominant in water. • Long-chain PFAS (>8 carbons) were predominant in sediments. • PFCAs were more persistent in surface waters than PFSAs. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: This study measured 17 perﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in surface water and sediments collected from six locations Received 3 April 2020 along the Las Vegas Wash and Lake Mead and eight locations along the Truckee River, Lake Tahoe, and Pyramid Lake Received in revised form 7 August 2020 in Nevada, United States. Of the 17 PFAS analyzed, 12 were detected in the surface water (n = 18) and 14 were detected Accepted 9 August 2020 in the sediments (n = 21) of the two watersheds. The total concentration of PFAS in the Truckee River water was Available online 15 August 2020 441.7 ng/L and the PFAS detected in the Las Vegas Wash water was 2234.3 ng/L. The predominant PFAS species Editor: Shuzhen Zhang found in the water were perﬂuorohexanoic acid (PFHxA) (1.5–187.0 ng/L), followed by perﬂuoropentanoic acid (PFPeA) (below detection limit [BDL] to 169.9 ng/L), perﬂuorooctanoic acid (PFOA) (BDL to 65.5 ng/L), and Keywords: perﬂuorobutane sulfonic acid (PFBS) (BDL to 44.7 ng/L). The total PFAS in the sediments was 272.9 μg/kg (dry weight) Perﬂuoroalkyl substances for the Truckee River and 345.7 μg/kg for the Las Vegas Wash. The predominant species in the sediments were PFAS perﬂuorodecane sulfonic acid (PFDS) (BDL to 88.2 μg/kg), PFHxA (BDL to 20.3 μg/kg), PFBS (BDL to 29.1 μg/Kg), and Perﬂuoroalkyl carboxylic acid perﬂuoroundecanoic acid (PFUA) (BDL to 22.9 μg/kg). The results demonstrated that short-chain PFAS (C ≤ 8) were Perﬂuoroalkyl sulfonic acid more prevalent in water, whereas long-chain PFAS (C > 8) were more detectable in sediments. The Las Vegas Wash Urban watershed water had much higher PFAS levels compared with the Truckee River water. The PFAS concentrations and detection frequencies also signiﬁcantly decreased in summer compared with winter along the Las Vegas Wash. © 2020 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Science of the Total Environment 751 (2021) 141767 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Science of the Total Environment journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv Legacy and emerging per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) in multi-media around a landﬁll in China: Implications for the usage of PFASs alternatives Chang Xu a,b, Zhaoyang Liu a, Xin Song a,b,⁎, Xiaoyan Ding a,b, Da Ding a,b a Key Laboratory of Soil Environment and Pollution Remediation, Institute of Soil Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Nanjing 210008, China b University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • 17 legacy and emerging PFASs in multi- media around a landﬁll were studied. • The landﬁll contributed to PFBA and PFPeA in surface water. • LogKd was negatively correlated with carbon chain lengths for short-chain PFCAs. • Short-chain PFASs, F-53B and 6:2 FTS were widely used in the region. • PFASs in groundwater via drinking posed low health risks to local residents. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: To date, per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) in environmental media surrounding landﬁlls have not Received 3 June 2020 attracted much attention. In this study, six legacy PFASs, six short-chain analogues and ﬁve emerging alternatives Received in revised form 16 August 2020 were investigated in groundwater, surface water and sediment samples taken in the vicinity of a valley-type landﬁll, Accepted 16 August 2020 which had been in operation for over 20 years. Total PFAS concentrations of 110–236 ng/L, 17.3–163 ng/L and Available online 18 August 2020 7.91–164 ng/g dw were detected in the surface water, groundwater and sediment samples, respectively. Overall, Editor: Shuzhen Zhang perﬂuorooctanoic acid (PFOA) was the dominant PFAS in surface water, but elevated concentrations of perﬂuorobutanoic acid (PFBA) and perﬂuoropentanoic acid (PFPeA) were detected in the surface water samples Keywords: taken adjacent to the landﬁll, suggesting that the landﬁll contributed to these compounds. PFBA was the dominant PFASs PFAS in the groundwater and sediments. The concentrations of long-chain perﬂuoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCAs) Environmental media (C8–C12) in the sediment samples correlated signiﬁcantly with the TOC, Al2O3 and Fe2O3 contents. The partitioning Landﬁll behaviors of PFCAs in the water-sediment system varied depending on the CF2 moiety units. For the long-chain Short-chain analogues PFCAs, positive correlations between the average LogKd and the number of CF2 moieties were found to be statisti- Health risks cally signiﬁcant, whereas negative correlations were observed for the short-chain PFCAs (C4–C7). The ratios of short-chain analogues and emerging alternatives versus their respective legacy PFOA and perﬂuorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) in surface water suggested an increasing trend of short-chain analogues, such as PFBA. The potential health risks of PFOS and PFOA, determined by calculating the estimated daily intake (EDI), were found to be negligible via the drinking groundwater exposure pathway, but more comprehensive studies on the human health risks of PFASs from landﬁlls are essential. © 2020 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. ⁎ Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Soil Environment and Pollution Remediation, Institute of Soil Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Nanjing 210008, China. pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Science of the Total Environment 781 (2021) 146747 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Science of the Total Environment journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/scitotenv Occurrence and sources of per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances in the ice-melting lakes of Larsemann Hills, East Antarctica Guoqiang Shan 1, Qian Xiang 1, Xuemin Feng, Wei Wu, Liping Yang, Lingyan Zhu ⁎ Key Laboratory of Pollution Processes and Environmental Criteria, Ministry of Education, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Environmental Remediation and Pollution Control, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Nankai University, Tianjin 300350, PR China H I G H L I G H T S G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T • The overall pollution of PFASs in East Antarctica was weaker than West Antarctica. • PFOA was predominant at tens to hun- dreds pg/L in the ice-melting lake water. • Isomer proﬁling and Unmix model were used to apportion the sources of PFASs in EA. • Air deposition was the main source of PFOA(S) in EA. • PFOA might be also contributed by local source. a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: The contamination and sources of per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFASs) in the Antarctic continent have Received 26 January 2021 not been systematically investigated. In this study, 21 PFASs including some new emerging one, were measured Received in revised form 21 March 2021 in the surface waters collected from 21 ice-melting lakes next to the research stations in Larsemann Hills, East Accepted 21 March 2021 Antarctica (EA). All the PFASs had a median concentration lower than 26.7 pg/L, representing the background Available online 26 March 2021 levels in EA. The contamination of PFASs in EA was generally lower than in West Antarctica (WA), which Editor: Shuzhen Zhang might be due to the less on-site human activities in EA than in WA. In the ice-melting lakes, perﬂuorooctane acid (PFOA) was predominant, and its concentrations in several lakes close to the research stations in EA could be up to 458 pg/L. For the ﬁrst time, an emerging substitute of perﬂuorooctane sulfate (PFOS), 6:2 chlorinated Keywords: polyﬂuorinated ether sulfonate (Cl-PFESA), was detected in several of the samples. Source apportionment Antarctica methods including isomer proﬁling were applied, and the results collectively indicated that the PFASs in the Ice-melting lake melting lakes in EA were mainly derived from airborne input, but local discharge might also contribute to PFASs PFOA in some lakes. The results of this study supplied information about the sources of PFASs in Antarctica, PFOA and suggested that caution should be taken in future to control the local discharge due to increasing human ac- 6:2 Cl-PFESA tivities in EA. pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Talanta 85 (2011) 345–352 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Talanta journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/talanta Quantitative detection of trace perﬂuorinated compounds in environmental water samples by Matrix-assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization-Time of Flight Mass Spectrometry with 1,8-bis(tetramethylguanidino)-naphthalene as matrix Dong Cao a , Zhidong Wang b,1 , Chunguang Han b,1 , Lin Cui a , Ming Hu b , Jingjing Wu a , Yongxue Liu b , Yaqi Cai a , Hailin Wang a , Yuehui Kang a,∗ a State Key Laboratory of Environmental Chemistry and Ecotoxicology, Research Center for Eco-Environmental Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, P.O. Box 2871, 18 Shuangqing Road, Haidian District, Beijing 100085, China b Beijing Institute of Radiation Medicine, 27 Taiping Road, Beijing 100850, China a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: Determination of perﬂuorinated compounds (PFCs) is very important because of their potential hazards Received 14 December 2010 to the environment and human health. In present work, 1,8-bis (tetramethylguanidino)-naphthalene Received in revised form 22 March 2011 (TMGN), a superbasic proton sponge, was ﬁrstly employed as the matrix for quantitative detection of Accepted 25 March 2011 acidic PFCs in environmental water samples by Matrix-assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization-Time of Available online 5 April 2011 Flight Mass Spectrometry (MALDI-TOF-MS). Several acidic PFCs, such as perﬂuorooctanesulfonate (PFOS) and perﬂuorooctanoic acid (PFOA), were selected as model analytes for demonstrating the feasibility of Keywords: the detection method. The results showed that deprotonated ions of these PFCs were detected without Perﬂuorinated compounds MALDI-TOF-MS any other matrix ions interference. The achieved sensitivity with TMGN for PFOS detection was ten-fold 1,8-Bis(tetramethylguanidino)naphthalene higher than that with 1,8-bis (dimethyl-amino)-naphthalene (DMAN) which was used for the detection Environmental analysis of fatty acid by MALDI-TOF-MS. The high sensitivity of this method made it feasible to monitor and quan- tify acidic PFCs in complicated environmental water samples. Furthermore, a novel combined strategy of solid phase extraction (SPE) followed by MALDI-TOF-MS detection was developed for quantifying PFCs in environmental water samples. The calibration curves with a wide linear dynamic range (0.1–10 ng L−1 for PFOS, PFHxS, and PFBS, and 0.5–50 ng L−1 for PFOA, PFNA, and PFDA.) were obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) for PFOS of this method was 0.015 ng L−1 (a signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which was lower than the LOD (0.036 ng L−1 ) obtained by high-pressure liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrome- try (LC–MS/MS) method. Moreover, the strategy was used to detect the selected PFCs in water samples collected from Xiaoqinghe river and Gaobeidian wastewater. The achieved concentrations of PFCs were closed to those obtained by LC–MS/MS method. It is indicated that the proposed MALDI-TOF-MS method with TMGN as the matrix is much reliable and can be used as an alternative method to detect trace PFCs in environmental water samples. © 2011 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction can exist for a long time and have bioaccumulation properties and toxic effects [2–5]. Hayworth Department of Civil Engineering, Auburn University, Auburn, AL 36849, United States A R T I C LE I N FO A B S T R A C T Keywords: Per- and polyﬂuoroalkyl substances (PFAS) represent a large group of synthetic organic compounds which, as a UHPLC-MS/MS result of their unique chemical properties, render them extremely recalcitrant to environmental degradation. Perﬂuoroalkyl substances Research concerning the environmental, ecological, and human health eﬀects of PFAS has focused on long Perﬂuoroethers aliphatic chain (> C7) compounds having no ether bonds. For new, less studied, or previously unknown PFAS Estuarine (≤ C7 with ether bonds), there is little to no information about their environmental behavior, transport, fate, exposure, and toxicological eﬀects. LC-MS/MS has proven eﬀective for detection and quantitation of some PFAS, however, straightforward analytical methods for simultaneous trace quantitation of broad mixtures of PFAS in varied complex environmental media, available to a wide range of researchers and also suitable for routine monitoring, remain critical needs. Here we describe a simple, rapid, sensitive, and reproducible quantitative analytical method for trace analysis and monitoring of 23 PFAS in estuarine water, using ultra-high performance liquid chromatography-triple quadrupole mass spectrometry (UHPLC-MS/MS). The developed MRM method allows simultaneous trace quantitation of a broad mixture of PFAS, including 13 perﬂuoroalkyl carboxylic acids, 8 perﬂuoroalkyl sulfonates, and 2 short-chain perﬂuoroethers. The method provides better peak resolution and peak separation, and shorter run times (method separation/total run time: 6/8 min) compared to those of ex- isting analytical methods. Percent recoveries for the validated method ranged from 78.54 to 112.61. LOD and LOQ values ranged from 0.48 to 1.68 pg/injection and 1.71 to 5.40 pg/injection, respectively. The validated method was used for quantitative PFAS analysis of estuarine water samples collected from 16 locations within the Perdido Bay estuary in coastal Alabama. 1. The fates of PFCs in the WWTPs Sewage sludge were related with the functional groups. The PFOS concentrations tended to decrease after LC/MS/MS treatment in most WWTPs, whereas PFOA increased. The different fates of PFOA and PFOS in WWTPs were attributed to the higher organic carbon-normalized distribution coefficient of perfluoroalkylsulfonate (PFASs) than that of the carboxylate analog, indicating the preference of PFASs to partition to sludge. This study aimed to investigate the 18 October 2016 occurrence and removal efﬁciency of eighteen PFASs in wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) and Accepted 18 October 2016 Available online 19 October 2016 drinking water plants (DWTPs) with different treatment processes. The results showed that both per- ﬂuorobutane sulfonic acid (PFBS) and perﬂuorooctane sulfonic acid (PFOS) were the predominant compounds in the water phase of WWTPs and DWTPs, while PFOS was dominant in dewatered sludge of Keywords: Perﬂuoroalkyl substances WWTPs. The average total PFASs concentrations in the three selected WWTPs were 19.6e232 ng/L in Wastewater inﬂuents, 15.5e234 ng/L in efﬂuents, and 31.5e49.1 ng/g dry weight in sludge. The distribution pattern of Drinking water PFASs differed between the wastewater and sludge samples, indicating strong partition of PFASs with Mass load long carbon chains to sludge. In the WWTPs, most PFASs were not eliminated efﬁciently in conventional Health risks activated sludge treatment, while the membrane bio-reactor (MBR) and Unitank removed approximately 50% of long chain (C  8) perﬂuorocarboxylic acids (PFCAs). The daily mass loads of total PFASs in WWTPs were in the range of 1956e24773 mg in inﬂuent and 1548e25085 mg in efﬂuent. PFASs were found at higher concentrations in the wastewater from plant A with some industrial wastewater input than from the other two plants (plant B and plant C) with mainly domestic wastewater sources. Meanwhile, the average total PFASs concentrations in the two selected DWTPs were detected at 4.74 e14.3 ng/L in the inﬂuent and 3.34e13.9 ng/L in the efﬂuent. In DWTPs, only granular activated carbon (GAC) and powder activated carbon (PAC) showed signiﬁcant removal of PFASs. The PFASs detected in the tap water would not pose immediate health risks in the short term exposure. The ﬁndings from this study showed that effective treatment technology should be applied to eliminate this group of chemicals in the urban water cycle based on the precautionary principle. © 2016 Elsevier Ltd. In this study, wastewater samples were collected from 17 WWTPs in Received in revised form 17 cities of China to investigate emerging PFASs in WWTPs. To comprehensively identify PFASs in the 25 May 2020 wastewater samples, an integrated suspect screening, homologue-based and fragment-based non-target Accepted 26 May 2020 Available online 19 June 2020 screening method is proposed. Sixty-three PFASs from 13 classes (25 subclasses) were identiﬁed, including 14 legacy and 49 emerging PFASs, and this study is the ﬁrst to report on 12 of these PFASs. We found that emerging PFASs concentration had a signiﬁcantly positive correlation with the gross domestic Keywords: Emerging PFASs product, indicating more substitution of legacy PFASs in the developed area of China. pdf_candidates.path: /mnt/d/paper_data/pdf/Nature/10.1002_pts.2140__Surma 等 - 2015 - Determination of Selected Perfluorinated Acids (PFCAs) and Perfluorinated Sulfonates (PFASs) in Food.pdf
pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY AND SCIENCE Packag. Technol. Sci. 2015; 28: 789–799 Published online 29 June 2015 in Wiley Online Library (wileyonlinelibrary.com) DOI: 10.1002/pts.2140 Determination of Selected Perﬂuorinated Acids (PFCAs) and Perﬂuorinated Sulfonates (PFASs) in Food Contact Materials Using LC-MS/MS By M. Surma,1* W. Wiczkowski,2 H. Zieliński2 and E. Cieślik1 1 Malopolska Centre of Food Monitoring, Faculty of Food Technology, University of Agriculture in Krakow, ul. Balicka 122, 30-149, Krakow, Poland 2 Department of Chemistry and Biodynamics of Food, Division of Food Science, Institute of Animal Reproduction and Food Research of the Polish Academy of Sciences, ul. Tuwima 10, 10-748, Olsztyn, Poland Perﬂuorinated compounds, including perﬂuorinated acids and perﬂuorinated sulfonates, are environ- mentally persistent. These compounds are commonly used in consumer products as stain/water/grease repellents in carpets and clothing, in cooking utensils as nonstick coating and in food contact materials as a coating. This paper explores the potential application of liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) system for the measurement of selected perﬂuorinated acids and perﬂuorinated sulfonates in three different brands (A, B and C) of food contact materials, including wrapping papers, breakfast bags, baking papers and roasting bags. In this study, the highest contents of selected perﬂuorinated acids were identiﬁed for the breakfast bag samples (2.54–6.60 pg/cm2), especially for B and C brands with the concentration of 6.60 and 5.35 pg/cm2, respectively, while the lowest content was noted in roasting bag samples (0.27–0.40 pg/cm2). The contents of perﬂuorinated sulfonates were inversed as compared with those of perﬂuorinated acids. The highest contents of selected perﬂuorinated sulfonates were found in roasting bag samples (1.38–5.17 pg/cm2), especially for B brand. The analysed perﬂuorinated sulfonates were not detected in any of the breakfast bag samples. The highest content of perﬂuorinated compounds were reported for B brand food contact materials. The data indicate a risk of food contamination by perﬂuorinated compounds from the commonly used food contact materials. Copyright © 2015 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. Received 28 November 2014; Revised 17 April 2015; Accepted 11 May 2015 KEY WORDS: perﬂuorinated compounds (PFCs); PFCAs; PFASs; food contact materials; LC-MS/MS INTRODUCTION Perﬂuorinated acids (PFCAs) and perﬂuorinated sulfonates (PFASs) belong to the family group of perﬂuorinated compounds (PFCs). Because of the strength of the C–F bond, they are chemically very stable and highly resistant to biological degradation. Therefore, they belong to a class of compounds that tend to persist in the environment and are proposed as a new class of persistent organic pollutants.1 Within the class of PFCs, perﬂuorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perﬂuorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) are generally considered reference substances.1 They have been most intensively studied from a toxicolog- ical standpoint as they are toxic, highly persistent and bioaccumulative. Human exposure to PFCs is due to a variety of environmental and product-related sources, although food (drinking water included) represents the dominant intake pathway. PFCs can also enter the body by ingestion of dust and dirt particles and by contact with products that have been treated with substances that contain PFCs or * Correspondence to: M. Surma, Malopolska Centre of Food Monitoring, Faculty of Food Technology, University of Agriculture in Krakow, ul. Balicka 122, 30–149 Krakow, Poland. E-mail: m.surma@ur.krakow.pl Copyright © 2015 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. 10991522, 2015, 9, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/pts.2140 by Fudan University, Wiley Online Library on [12/03/2026]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License 790 M. SURMA ET AL. its precursor compounds.2,3 Another important route is the inhalation of PFCs in indoor or outdoor air, as well as the inhalation of waterprooﬁng sprays. Also, dermal exposure may occur by skin contact with PFC-treated products. 4–6 Therefore, human exposure to PFCs is currently receiving considerable attention from scientist and policymakers because of the ubiquity of these substances in human blood and tissue samples worldwide, but particularly in industrialized areas. PFOA are one of the two PFCs that have been recently reported at ppb levels in serum of the general population in many countries. Perﬂuorinated compounds are routinely used in industry as stain–resistant and water–resistant coatings for fabrics, carpets and clothing; in ﬁreﬁghting foam; in cooking utensils as nonstick coatings; or in food contact material as a coating (e.g. baking paper to prevent oil leaching into the paper).7,8 When coming into contact with foods, paper and cardboard packaging is protected from softening by treatment with, among other things, water-resistant and oil-resistant perﬂuorochemicals. Contaminants in the coatings ﬂuorotelomer alcohols can be present, and ~1% of them can be converted to PFOA in the body.9,10 Furthermore, PFOA is used in the production of polytetraﬂuoroethylene nonstick surface coatings for cooking utensils or paper coatings, and therefore, it may be present in residual amounts.11 Powley et al.12 were unable to detect a migration of PFOA from PFTE-coated cooking utensils [limit of detection (LOD) 0.1 ng/cm2]. Begley et al.13 showed that nonstick cooking utensils contribute less to PFC exposure to food than coated papers or cardboard boxes. Residual amounts of PFOA in the range of a few nanograms per kilogram were all that could be detected in polytetraﬂuoroethylene cooking utensils. In contrast, some paper and cardboard surface coatings contained large amounts of PFCs. Microwave popcorn bags were found

pdf_candidates.path: /mnt/d/paper_data/pdf/Nature/10.1007_s00216-019-01829-8__Coggan 等 - 2019 - A single analytical method for the determination of 53 legacy and emerging per- and polyfluoroalkyl.pdf
pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Analytical and Bioanalytical Chemistry (2019) 411:3507–3520 https://doi.org/10.1007/s00216-019-01829-8 RESEARCH PAPER A single analytical method for the determination of 53 legacy and emerging per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) in aqueous matrices Timothy L. Coggan 1 2 2 1 & Tarun Anumol & James Pyke & Jeff Shimeta & Bradley O. Clarke 1 Received: 30 November 2018 / Revised: 27 February 2019 / Accepted: 3 April 2019 / Published online: 9 May 2019 # Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2019 Abstract A quantitative method for the determination of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) using liquid chromatography (LC) tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS) was developed and applied to aqueous wastewater, surface water, and drinking water samples. Fifty-three PFAS from 14 compound classes (including many contaminants of emerging concern) were measured using a single analytical method. After solid-phase extraction using weak anion exchange cartridges, method detection limits in water ranged from 0.28 to 18 ng/L and method quantitation limits ranged from 0.35 to 26 ng/L. Method accuracy ranged from 70 to 127% for 49 of the 53 extracted PFAS, with the remaining four between 66 and 138%. Method precision ranged from 2 to 28% RSD, with 49 out of the 53 PFAS being below < 20%. In addition to quantifying > 50 PFAS, many of which are currently unregulated in the environment and not included in typical analytical lists, this method has efficiency advantages over other similar methods as it utilizes a single chromatographic separation with a shorter runtime (14 min), while maintaining method accuracy and stability and the separation of branched and linear PFAS isomers. The method was applied to wastewater influent and effluent; surface water from a river, wetland, and lake; and drinking water samples to survey PFAS contamination in Australian aqueous matrices. The compound classes FTCAs, FOSAAs, PFPAs, and diPAPs were detected for the first time in Australian WWTPs and the method was used to quantify PFAS concentrations from 0.60 to 193 ng/L. The range of compound classes detected and different PFAS signatures between sample locations demonstrate the need for expanded quantitation lists when investigating PFAS, especially newer classes in aqueous environmental samples. pdf_candidates.path: /mnt/d/paper_data/pdf/Nature/10.1007_s00244-017-0474-x__Dong 等 - 2018 - Occurrence and Partition of Perfluorinated Compounds (PFCs) in Water and Sediment from the Songhua R.pdf
pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Archives of Environmental Contamination and Toxicology (2018) 74:492–501 https://doi.org/10.1007/s00244-017-0474-x Occurrence and Partition of Perfluorinated Compounds (PFCs) in Water and Sediment from the Songhua River, China Weihua Dong1 · Baolin Liu2 · Yang Song1 · Hong Zhang3 · Juying Li4 · Xiaoyu Cui3 Received: 10 May 2017 / Accepted: 1 November 2017 / Published online: 17 November 2017 © Springer Science+Business Media, LLC 2017 Abstract This study provided the first evidence that perfluorinated compounds (PFCs) were widely detected in the Songhua River, China. Seventeen surface water and sediment samples were collected and analyzed for the determination of 14 PFCs. The total concentrations of PFCs (Σ PFCs) ranged from 0.143 to 1.41 ng L−1 in water samples. Perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) was detected with the highest detection frequency (%) ranging from below LOQ to 0.678 ng L−1. Σ PFCs were relatively low in sediments, and only four individual homologues were detected. Perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) and PFOA were detected with the lowest levels in this study compared with other PFCs detected in all the rivers of China in previous stud- ies. The concentrations of PFCs were highly influenced by distribution of wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs). The effluents from WWTPs, which are discharged into the Songhua River, are regarded as the main contamination sources of PFCs in this study. Even though low risk for the concentrations of PFOS and PFOA to aquatic ecosystem of the Songhua River was found in the analysis of potential adverse effect, further experimental studies on occurrence of PFCs and their potential adverse effects to wildlife and humans should be conducted continuously in the Songhua River basin because of the increasing discharge. The mean partition coefficients (log Koc) of PFOS between sediment and water was 4.49 cm3 g−1, which was probably influenced by the sediment characteristics and hydrodynamic parameters. PFCs tend to accumulate in water compared with other persistent organic pollutants. Perfluorinated compounds (PFCs) have been abundantly and Kannan 2001, 2002). Previous studies have indicated produced and widely used in lubricants, textile, firefight- that PFCs were ubiquitously detected in the environmental ing foams, leather, furniture manufacture, plastics, rubber, matrices including water (Ahrens 2011; Bao et al. 2012; etc. (Kissa 2001; Giesy and Kannan 2002; Paul et al. 2009; Post et al. 2013; Rayne and Forest 2009; Yamashita et al. Prevedouros et al. 2006). PFCs are usually resistant to deg- 2005), air (Chaemfa et al. 2010; Dreyer et al. 2009; Li et al. radation due to the strong stability of C–F covalent bonds 2011a, b; Liu et al. 2015a), soil (Kim et al. 2014; Wang and have received extensive concern worldwide (Giesy et al. 2013b), and wild lives (Houde et al. 2006). In addi- tion, PFCs are unexceptionally detected in the blood and serum of human (Bao et al. 2011; Kannan et al. 2004; Olsen Electronic supplementary material The online version of this et al. 2003). pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Archives of Environmental Contamination and Toxicology (2019) 76:206–215 https://doi.org/10.1007/s00244-018-0585-z Poly‑ and Perfluoroalkyl Substances in Runoff Water and Wastewater Sampled at a Firefighter Training Area Xavier Dauchy1 · Virginie Boiteux1 · Adeline Colin1 · Cristina Bach1 · Christophe Rosin1 · Jean‑François Munoz1 Received: 2 October 2018 / Accepted: 27 November 2018 / Published online: 4 December 2018 © Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2018 Abstract Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) are key ingredients of firefighting foams designed to suppress fires involv- ing flammable and combustible liquids. Such foams are used by firefighters during fire training at dedicated sites. Because PFASs are very persistent chemicals, substantial soil and groundwater contamination has been observed in the vicinity of firefighter training areas. However, very few data are available on PFAS contamination of wastewater and runoff water on such sites. The purpose of this study was to evaluate the occurrence of more than 50 PFASs in 43 water samples (effluent from a wastewater treatment plant (WWTP), lagoon, runoff water, and wastewater drained from firefighter training areas) collected from a large firefighter training site, using target and suspect screening. A comparison of the PFAS classes analyzed revealed the overwhelming contribution of fluorotelomers. This indicates that the PFAS emission from the use of firefighting foams cannot be monitored only by measuring perfluoroalkyl acids. Based on the PFAS concentrations measured through target screening, the PFAS mass discharged into the river receiving WWTP effluent and the lagoon was on average 387 ± 183 kg and 56 ± 15 kg per year respectively. Due to the unavailability of standards, it was impossible to take into account the PFASs detected with suspect screening. The present study emphasizes that, above and beyond soil and groundwater contamination, such sites also contribute to the PFAS burden of surface water. pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Archives of Environmental Contamination and Toxicology (2021) 81:133–141 https://doi.org/10.1007/s00244-021-00855-x Occurrence and Ecological Risk Assessment of Perfluoroalkyl Substances (PFASs) in Water and Sediment from an Urban River in South China Yuanyuan Si1 · Jiankun Huang2 · Zhihong Liang3 · Guoqiang Liu2 · Da Chen2 · Ying Guo2 · Fei Wang2 Received: 27 September 2020 / Accepted: 2 May 2021 / Published online: 26 May 2021 © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2021 Abstract The chemical substances in urban rivers influence municipal water systems and reflect the recent use of these chemicals by humans or industries around the urban center. In this study, seven perfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs)—perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA), perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA), 2-per- fluorohexyl ethanol (6:2 FTOH), 2-perfluorooctyl ethanol (8:2 FTOH), and 6:2 chlorinated polyfluoroalkyl ether sulfonic acids (F-53B)—could be detected and quantified in river water and sediment samples collected from one tributary of the Liuxi River, which is part of Pearl River near Guangzhou in Guangdong province, South China. The fluxes of target PFASs into Liuxi River and their related ecological risks were further estimated. The total concentrations of PFASs (ΣPFASs) ranged from 506 to 3.16 × ­103 ng/L in water samples and 9.13 to 850 ng/L in sediment samples. The two dominant PFAS compounds were 6:2 FTOH and PFHpA, which accounted for more than 90.0% of ΣPFASs in river water and sediment. Cor- relation analysis showed that there was significant positive correlation (p < 0.01) between two selected PFASs (e.g., between 6:2 FTOH and PFHpA). Correlation analysis of PFASs in river water and sediment indicated most PFASs in sediment were partitioned from river water. The ecological risk assessment indicated that the detected PFASs have a low risk (HQ < 0.1) in river water and sediment to Daphnia magna in the Liuxi River.
